votes.reason: The Python function correctly checks if the boolean 'res' matches the condition that all elements in 'l' are strictly less than 't', which corresponds exactly to the Coq specification.
The Python code correctly checks if 'res' matches the boolean evaluation of whether all elements in 'l' are strictly less than 't', which is exactly what the Coq specification requires.
The Python code correctly checks if 'res' matches the boolean value of whether all elements in 'l' are strictly less than 't', which is exactly what the Coq specification requires.

The Python code returns True iff all elements in l are strictly less than t, which matches the Coq spec: res=true iff Forall (fun x => Z.lt x t) l, and res=false iff there exists an element x in l with t <= x. Both conditions are logically equivalent to checking whether all elements are below the threshold.

The Python code returns True iff all elements in l are strictly less than t, which matches the Coq spec: res=true iff Forall (fun x => Z.lt x t) l, and res=false iff exists x in l with t <= x. Both conditions in the Coq spec are logically equivalent to res == all(x < t for x in l).

The Python code returns True iff all elements in l are strictly less than t, which matches the Coq spec: res=true iff Forall (fun x => Z.lt x t) l, and res=false iff exists x in l with t <= x. These are logically equivalent characterizations of the same boolean result.

Both require res to be true iff all elements of l are strictly less than t (and false iff some element is >= t). The Python check res == all(x < t for x in l) matches the Coq bi-implication on all concrete inputs with integer lists.

Both require res to be true iff all elements of l are < t; the Coq second clause is the complementary condition (exists x >= t) which is equivalent in Z, so the Python check matches the spec on all inputs.
It checks exactly that res equals whether all elements of l are < t, which matches the Coq spec (true iff Forall x<t, false iff ∃x≥t).
